coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of AgPt nano-pompons for efficient methanol oxidation: Morphology control and DFT study on stability enhancement
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry

Bai, Dehong

He, Shijie

Zhou, Xibin
coredata.dc:description: Facile synthesis of more dendritic and uniform Pt-based nanostructures with carbon materials could greatly reduce cost and increase Pt utilization for methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) in direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC). This study reports a novel one-pot method to fabricate AgPt nano-pompons (AgPt NPs) with the guidance of N-GQDs through AA reduce the precursor of Ag and Pt. Morphology characterization describes N-GQDs as morphology guiding and dispersing agents to regulate the dendrite formation of nano-pompons. Under the optimized conditions, the AgPt NPs (Ag1Pt2) display above 11 times improvement in electrocatalytic activity and higher stability for the MOR compared with Pt/C catalysts. Density function theory (DFT) studies prove that the introduction of Ag can greatly enhance the adsorption of CO on Pt and promote the transformation of CO to COOH. The facile synthetic method and excellent MOR performance endow AgPt NPs with great application prospect in DMFCs as an anode catalyst.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N doping graphene quantum dots
Silver-platinum alloy
Nano-pompons
Methanol oxidation reaction
Bifunctional mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The effect of composition on CO adsorption

item-info.ce:copyright: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N-GQDs; (b, c) TEM and (d) HRTEM images of AgPt NPs; (e) SAED and (f) XRD patterns of AgPt NPs; Inset: the related size distribution.
(a)HAADF-STEM image, (b) EDS mapping, (c) EDS spectrum, and (d) line scanning profiles of AgPt NPs.
(a) Survey and high-resolution (b) C1s, (c) N 1 s, (d) O 1 s, (e) Ag 3d, and (f) Pt 4f XPS spectra of AgPt NPs.

(a and d) TEM, (b and e) HRTEM images, and (c and f) SAED patterns of the reaction products obtained by the addition of varying amount of N-GQDs during synthesis: (a, b, and c) 0 μL and (d, e, and f) 400 μL.
(a, b) TEM images, (c) SAED patterns, (d) EDS spectrum, and (e) line scanning profiles of the hollow N-GQDs/AgPt nanoparticles obtained by change the order of adding AA and H2PtCl6.
Schematic illustration of the formation mechanism of AgPt NPs.

CV curves (a) in 1.0 M NaOH solution and (b) 1.0 M NaOH + 0.5 M methanol solution, (c) contrast histograms of specific activity and mass activity, (d) CA curves in 1.0 M NaOH + 0.5 M CH3OH solution, and (e) EIS Nyquist plots in 0.1 M K3[Fe(CN)6] + 0.1 M K4[Fe(CN)4] + 1.0 M KCl solution for different Ag/Pt mole ratios of AgPt NPs and Pt/C catalysts.
The TODS of (a) Ag, Pt, and AgPt alloys; the TDOS and PDOS of (b) CO adsorption to Pt (1 1 1), and (c) AgPt alloy (1 1 1) crystal plane.
Charge density of Mulliken population distribution: (a) CO on Pt (1 1 1) and (b) AgPt (1 1 1).
The adsorption energy (Ead), the Pt-C distance (d), bond length of CO molecule after optimization (l) and the Mulliken charge (ΔQ) of CO molecule on Pt (1 1 1) and AgPt (1 1 1) crystal plane.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of AgPt nano-pompons for efficient methanol oxidation: Morphology control and DFT study on stability enhancement

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Resource Environment and Sustainable Development of Oasis, Gansu Province, College of Geography and Environment Science, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Resource Environment and Sustainable Development of Oasis
College of Geography and Environment Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Resource Environment and Sustainable Development of Oasis, Gansu Province, College of Geography and Environment Science, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Facile synthesis of more dendritic and uniform Pt-based nanostructures with carbon materials could greatly reduce cost and increase Pt utilization for methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) in direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC). This study reports a novel one-pot method to fabricate AgPt nano-pompons (AgPt NPs) with the guidance of N-GQDs through AA reduce the precursor of Ag and Pt. Morphology characterization describes N-GQDs as morphology guiding and dispersing agents to regulate the dendrite formation of nano-pompons. Under the optimized conditions, the AgPt NPs (Ag1Pt2) display above 11 times improvement in electrocatalytic activity and higher stability for the MOR compared with Pt/C catalysts. Density function theory (DFT) studies prove that the introduction of Ag can greatly enhance the adsorption of CO on Pt and promote the transformation of CO to COOH. The facile synthetic method and excellent MOR performance endow AgPt NPs with great application prospect in DMFCs as an anode catalyst.

ce:section.ce:para: Direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC) has enormous potential as an energy conversion equipment owing to its high theoretical energy conversion efficiency (97 %), low environmental pollution, and easy storage [1–5]. Platinum (Pt) has been discovered as the most effective catalyst for the methanol oxidation reactive (MOR) of DMFC. However, the high price and rarity of pure Pt limit the widespread deployment of DMFC in practical application [6–10]. Moreover, the MOR mainly consists of continuous dehydrogenation of methanol, yielding CO species absorb onto the Pt surface (Pt-COads) and poison the Pt surface via encroaching catalytically active sites, resulting in a serious reduction in catalytic efficiency [11–13]. The development of a high active electrocatalyst that minimize the dosage of Pt necessary and enhances the CO tolerance of Pt for MOR is the pursuit of researchers.

Pt can be combined with cheaper transition metal (M) such as Ni [14,15], Ag [16], or Co [17] et al. to improve the CO tolerance and catalytic activity for MOR [18]. There are two main effects of doping other atoms to improve the CO tolerance of Pt, one is ligand effect that doping M atoms can weaken the adsorption strength of CO on Pt. For instance, concave PtCo nanocrosses reported by Li et al. exhibited outstanding reaction kinetics of MOR, due to the ligand effect between Co and Pt atoms weakens the Pt-COads adsorption energy, which is conductive to CO desorption from Pt surface [19]. The other is bifunctional mechanism that promoted the transformation of CO to COOH. Ishikawa et al. demonstrated that doping Sn, Ru, and Ge of Pt promotes greater dissociation of H2O into ·OH than pure Pt, which facilitates that the conversion of CO to COOH [20]. Silver (Ag) as an ideal metal doped with Pt not only reduces cost of electrocatalysts but also more easily to alloy with Pt than other transition-metals (Cu, Ni, and Co et al.) [21–23]. But at the moment, there have been few studies have to research the mechanism of anti-CO poisoning on the surface of AgPt alloy for MOR.

It is known that the MOR active sites are mainly located on the surface of electrocatalysts so that the surface structure is an important factor in evaluating the performance of electrocatalytic [24]. Increasing the exposure of active sites on Pt surface via controlling the composition, size, and morphology of electrocatalysts can efficiently promote MOR activity. Hence, a variety of Pt-based nanostructures were developed, including core–shell [25], porous [26], hollow dendrites [27], and nano-pompons [28]. Among these morphologies, the nano-pompons have attracted plenty of attention owing to their higher specific surface area and low coordination number atoms at the edges, providing more reaction sites for the adsorbates involved in a close range [29]. Traditional synthesis strategies of nano-pompons are usually based on seed growth methods, which are complication and time-consuming. A facile and general method is desirable for the large-scaled preparation of novel Pt-based nano-pompons catalysts. Moreover, the introduction of carbon-based materials as the suitable morphology guiding agents and dispersing agents is an effective method to regulate the shape, size, and structure. Among the carbon-based materials, N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have more functional groups, effective tuning of electronic, and excellent assist in dispersing the metal nanomaterials than GQDs [30,31]. Meanwhile, the N-GQDs have been reported that it is conducive to providing many anchoring sites for the adsorption of metal ions and supplying more active sites for the MOR [32,33].

Herein, we develop a facile one-pot method at room temperature to fabricate silver-platinum nano-pompons (AgPt NPs) with the guidance of N-GQDs. In this process, N-GQDs as morphology guiding and dispersing agents to prepare homogeneous dendrite structures. Furthermore, the catalytic performance for MOR of AgPt NPs was evaluated in alkaline solution, and the measurements demonstrate that AgPt NPs exhibit enhanced MOR catalytic activity and long-term stability, which are better than commercial Pt/C. Density function theory (DFT) studies demonstrate that the adsorption of CO on AgPt alloy surface is enhanced compared with pure Pt surface, which is beneficial to the transformation of CO to COOH in the bifunctional mechanism.

All reagents used in this work were of analytical grade and all solutions were prepared using secondary distilled water. Silver nitrate (AgNO3), chloroplatinic acid hexahydrate (H2PtCl6·6H2O), citric acid, urea, ascorbic acid (AA) were purchased from Aladdin.

The N-GQDs were synthesized by referring to previous study [34], 1.7 g urea and 2.0 g citric acid were dissolved into 50 mL secondary distilled water at room temperature. Then, the above solution was transferred into 100 mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave and heated in an oven at 160 ℃ for 8 h. After cooling down at room temperature, the solution was dialyzed in a 3500 Da dialysis bag for 2 days.

AgPt NPs were fabricated by a facile one-pot method. Firstly, 120 μL of AgNO3 (10 mM) and 200 μL of N-GQDs (25 mM) were put into 20 mL H2O under stirring at 25 ℃, and then 120 μL of H2PtCl6 (19.3 mM) were added to the above solution. After 5 min, 1 mL of AA (0.1 M) were added into above mixture solution with continuous stir for 1 h. Then, 200 μL of NH3·H2O were added into above solution continued reacted for 2 h to obtain AgPt NPs (Ag1Pt2). Samples with different Ag/Pt mole of rate (Ag4Pt5, Ag3Pt5, and Ag2Pt5) were synthesized by varying the feed ratio AgNO3 and H2PtCl6.
AgPt nanoparticles were prepared in only change the order of adding AA and H2PtCl6 condition, keeping the other experimental conditions unchanged.

All electrochemical experiments were based on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation. A three-electrode system (a platinum wire as the auxiliary electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, and a catalyst modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE, Φ = 3 mm) as the working electrode) was used for all electrochemical tests. The catalyst inks were prepared via ultrasonically dispersing the mixture of 5 mg different ratios of Ag/Pt samples and 5 mL H2O, respectively. Then, 5 μL of above suspension liquid (1 mg/mL) was dropped onto the surface of a cleanly GCE and dried at 25 ℃.

Density functional theory (DFT) was calculated based on the Cambridge sequential total energy package (CASTEP) using the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) functional. The energy cutoff was set as 570 eV, and the total energy tolerance of 2 × 10−5 eV and force of 0.05 eV/Å were set as the convergence criteria. Meanwhile, the catalyst surface was cleaved into (1 1 1) surface and vacuum space was set as 15 Å to avoid periodic interactions. The CO adsorption models were constructed as the most stable bridge adsorption.

The morphologic and crystalline structures of the AgPt NPs were characterized with transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Typical TEM image (Fig. 1 a) of N-GQDs shows that they are dispersed uniformly with an average diameter of 2.67 ± 0.7 nm. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of N-GQDs (Fig. S1) displays two clear absorption peaks at 234 nm (π->π* of C = C) and 328 nm (n->π* of C = O) [34]. The TEM images of AgPt NPs, Fig. 1b and c, exhibit clear spiny dendrites and lots of little particles with an average size of 261.4 ± 44.7 nm. As described in Fig. 1d, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) characterization indicates that the lattice fringes are clearly appeared and the lattice spacing distances at marked position is 0.230 and 0.232 nm, which are well corresponded to the (1 1 1) crystal plans of face-centered cubic (fcc) AgPt alloy [35]. Moreover, the selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 1e) of AgPt NPs forcefully evidences its polycrystalline nature. X-ray diffraction (XRD) of AgPt NPs (Fig. 1f) shows these nanostructures have a fcc crystal structure with a broad diffraction peak at 20°-30° attributing to the graphite (002) planes [25], which manifests that the N-GQDs exist in this nanostructure. Moreover, there are four diffraction peaks at 38.90°, 45.52°, 65.78°, and 80.12° corresponds to the (1 1 1), (200), (220), and (311) crystal planes of the fcc AgPt alloy, respectively [36]. All the characteristic diffraction peaks are located between those of pure Ag (JCPDS No. 04–0783) and pure Pt (JCPDS No. 04–0802), demonstrating the formation of AgPt alloy phase.

High-angle annular dark field-scanning TEM (HAADF-STEM) images of AgPt (Fig. 2 a and b) further reveal the composition distribution. From the images, N, Ag, and Pt atoms are uniformly distribute throughout the nanostructure, proving there are present of N-GQDs in the entire nanostructures and the formation of AgPt alloy. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 2c, the correlated energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) spectrum displays that C, N, Ag, and Pt signals are detected apart from Cu atoms, which indicates that the high crystallinity and purity of AgPt NPs. The line scanning profile (Fig. 2d) more clearly displays that Ag and Pt elements coexist throughout all of the structure, further proving the formation of AgPt alloy.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is used to check the surface compositions and element chemical state of AgPt NPs [37]. Fig. 3 a depicts the survey XPS pattern, in which the nanostructure contains C, N, O, Ag, and Pt atoms, demonstrating the formation of AgPt NPs. Fig. 3b shows that the three typical peaks of high resolution XPS of C 1 s can be assigned to C–C (284.7 eV), C-O (286.1 eV), and O-CO (287.5 eV) functional groups.[38]. As shown in Fig. 3c, the high resolution N 1 s XPS spectrum displays that three peaks at 398.2 eV, 399.9 eV, and 402.6 eV corresponding to the pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively [39]. These N species usually have an reinforcing effect on electro-catalytic property [40]. Fig. 3d displays that the high resolution XPS of O 1 s is divided from the CO (532.6 eV) and C-O/C-O-C (535.4 eV) [41]. The two peaks of Ag 3d spectra (Fig. 3e) at 367.5 and 373.5 eV are consistent with the Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 orbits of metallic Ag0, indicating the efficient reduction of the AgNO3 [42]. Meanwhile, Fig. 3f shows the high resolution XPS spectrum of Pt 4f in which the stronger couple emerges at 71.3 (4f7/2) and 74.7 (4f5/2) eV are assigned to Pt0 [43], and the weaker couple at 72.1 (4f7/2) and 76.0 eV (4f5/2) should be corresponded to Pt2+ [44]. Moreover, compared with the binding energy of pure Ag (367.9 and 373.8 eV) and pure Pt (70.90 and 74.25 eV), the peaks of Ag 3d and Pt 4f shift to lower and higher binding energy, respectively. The shift of the binding energy indicates charge transfer between Ag and Pt owing to the formation of AgPt alloy [45,46].

To study the formation mechanism of AgPt NPs, a series of control experiments were conducted. The impact of N-GQDs on the formation of AgPt NPs was firstly investigated. When no N-GQDs were added to the system, as shown in Fig. 4 a, the reaction products are nanodendrites with a less dendrite and non-uniform shape rather than the more dendrite nano-pompons. Moreover, the HRTEM image (Fig. 4b) displays a lattice spacing of edge regions is 0.225 nm, which is corresponded to the (1 1 1) lattice planes of pure Pt, and another lattice spacing (0.230 nm) of internal regions is consistent with fcc (1 1 1) of AgPt alloy. The SAED pattern (Fig. 4c) shows a rather chaotic crystal structure. However, when 400 μL of N-GQDs were added to the reaction, long and irregular dendrites are generated (Fig. 4d), which can be attributed to the excessive N-GQDs make dendrites overgrowth. The lattice spacing (Fig. 4e) and SAED pattern (Fig. 4f) of the reaction products (400 μL N-GQDs) display a polycrystalline structure with AgPt (1 1 1) as the dominant crystal plane. These results demonstrate the importance of N-GQDs in fabricating high-quality AgPt NPs with abundant dendrites and the formation of AgPt alloy.

Besides, whether the galvanic replacement reaction can occur between Ag0 and Pt precursor (PtCl6 2− or PtCl4 2−) is distinctly one of the key factors in the synthesis of AgPt NPs. The Fig. 5 a and b show that when changing the order of adding AA and H2PtCl6, the morphology of reaction products shows a typical hollow structure. The SEAD pattern (Fig. 5c) shows that the hollow AgPt nanoparticles are polycrystalline structures. Meanwhile, the EDS spectrum (Fig. 5d) of the reaction products proves the presence of C, N, Ag, and Pt atoms. Line scanning profile (Fig. 5e) displays that the Pt and Ag signals are present throughout the crystal, and the contents of Ag and Pt are mainly distributed in the boundary layer. According to the above survey, when the injection of AA, the AgNO3 is reduced to metallic Ag nanoparticles. With PtCl6 2− added to the solution, the galvanic replacement reaction occurs between Ag and PtCl6 2− (Eq. 1) [47].
(1) 4Ag + PtCl6 2− → Pt + 4Ag+ + 6Cl−

The dissolution of Ag nanoparticles results in the formation of hollow structures. Then, the Ag+ ions are reduced to Ag atoms by excess AA, followed the newly formed Ag and Pt atoms continually fused together to formation AgPt alloy due to the high surface energies [27]. However, without hollow structures produced during the synthesis of AgPt NPs. It is maybe related to the difference in redox potentials between PtCl6 2− and PtCl4 2−. The Ag+ ions are more easily reduced than PtCl6 2− due to the redox potential of [PtCl6 2−/Pt] (1.47 V vs. SCE) is observably higher than that of [Ag+/Ag] (0.8 V vs. SCE). The galvanic replacement reaction between Ag0 and PtCl6 2− will produce the hollow structure, but the replacement reaction does not occur between Ag0 and PtCl4 2−, because of the redox potential of [PtCl4 2−/Pt] (0.76 V vs. SCE) is lower than that of [Ag+/Ag] [48]. Therefore, in the process of synthesis of AgPt NPs, the PtCl4 2− ions are formed before Ag0 elements after adding AA, and the AgPt alloys are synthesized by the co-reduction of Ag+ and PtCl4 2− ions.

Fig. 6 illustrates the formation mechanism of AgPt NPs. For the typical synthesis, the N-GQDs served as both structure-directing and dispersing agents because the various oxygen-containing groups on the surface provide a large number of active sites. Firstly, mixing the AgNO3, H2PtCl6 and AA solutions with N-GQDs, the PtCl6 2− ions are preferentially reduced to PtCl4 2− by the reducing agents of AA followed by releasing 2Cl─ into the solution, and the released Cl─ ions will quickly combine with part of Ag+ ions to formation AgCl precipitates [49]. Then, the Ag+ and PtCl4 2− ions are co-reduced to metallic Ag and Pt by the excess AA because of their similar redox potential. Meanwhile, the newly-generated Ag and Pt atoms with high surface free energies are rapidly covered by the adjacent N-GQDs. Then, the AgPt alloy cores are formed via crystal fusion of Ag and Pt atoms to reduce the surface free energies [50]. The dendrites of nano-pompons begin directional growth under the guidance of N-GQDs, as a result, AgPt NPs are formed by surface aggregation, self-assembly, Ostwald ripening process, and oriented-growth [51]. Moreover, the AgCl impurity adsorbed on the AgPt NPs is removed by adding NH3·H2O.

The catalytic active sites are provided by the exposed surface area of nano-pompons structures, thus to measure the number of active sites on the surface of the AgPt NPs, the cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurement was performed in 1.0 M NaOH solution at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. Voltammograms for different Ag/Pt mole ratios samples are shown for comparison. Calculating the electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) is performed by integrating the Coulombic charge of hydrogen adsorption and desorption region of these catalysts [52]. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the AgPt NPs with different Ag/Pt mole rotes show ECSA values of 7.0 m2g−1 (Ag1Pt2), 2.8 m2g−1 (Ag4Pt5), 3.9 m2g−1 (Ag3Pt5), and 1.5 m2g−1 (Ag2Pt5). Obviously, ECSA of Ag1Pt2 is the largest, which is significantly higher than commercial Pt/C catalysts (2.8 m2g−1). This high ECSA can be attributed to the more dendrites of nano-pompons morphology.

The electrocatalytic mass activity of different Ag/Pt mole ratios for the MOR reaction was tested via CV measurement in 1.0 M NaOH + 0.5 M CH3OH solution at a scan rate of 50 mV/s (Fig. 7b). The mass activity of Ag1Pt2 (1190.8 mA mgPt −1) is about 2.5 times higher than Ag4Pt5 (482.5 mA mgPt −1), 2.4 times higher than Ag3P5 (490.8 mA mgPt −1), 1.9 times higher than Ag2Pt5 (635.8 mA mgPt −1), and 11 times high than the commercial Pt/C (105.2 mA mgPt −1). Moreover, compared with other mole ratios, the Ag1Pt2 has a more negative initial potential during the onset of MOR, manifesting that MOR more easily occur on surface of this catalyst [19]. The Fig. 7c shows that the specific activity and mass activity of all four Ag/Pt mole ratios catalysts have much larger than commercial Pt/C electrocatalysts, manifesting an excellent intrinsic activity of the AgPt NPs. From the data in Table S1, our values are comparable with other results obtained for MOR in alkaline media. It was found that the AgPt NPs catalyst has a higher current density peak compared with the reported catalysts.

To evaluate the long-term MOR stability of the AgPt NPs, the chronoamperometry (CA) test of different Ag/Pt mole ratios (Fig. S3) and Pt/C catalysts (Fig. 7d) was implemented in 1.0 M NaOH + 0.5 M CH3OH solution for 3600 s at a potential of 0.6 V. All of the polarization currents for the MOR on the electrocatalysts show quick decay at the initial stage, due to the adsorption of poisonous species. However, the AgPt NPs electrocatalysts prepared with Ag1Pt2 take on a slower current decay rate and retain a higher mass activity than commercial Pt/C, which shows that optimize the mole ratios of Ag and Pt element can improve electrochemical stability. Moreover, the structure and morphology of the recovered AgPt NPs did not change after CA measurement (Fig. S2), demonstrating their morphological stability throughout MOR.

The specific process and kinetic of AgPt modified electrode were measured by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Normally, a smaller diameter of the semicircle part of the EIS curve represent a smaller charger-transfer resistance of the as-obtained electrocatalyst. As shown in Fig. 7e, the Ag1Pt2 has a smaller impedance arc than commercial Pt/C, indicating a better electrical conductivity for the MOR [53].

According to the above electrochemical performance test results, the excellent electrocatalytic performance of AgPt NPs for MOR can derive from the nano-pompons structure, doping of N-GQDs, and alloyed AgPt composition. The unique structures of AgPt NPs provide the remarkable enlargement of the ECSA, providing adequate active sites available for MOR [29]. The synergistic and electronic effects between Ag, Pt, and N atoms can facilitate charge transfer, resulting in the improvement of electrocatalytic performance [38]. Moreover, the AgPt alloy has high thermodynamic stability [54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of composition on CO adsorption
ce:section.ce:para:
To further research the anti-CO poisoning mechanism (ligand effect or bifunctional mechanism) for the AgPt alloy system, the density function theory (DFT) simulation was conducted to study the electron structure and reaction mechanism of CO adsorption on both Pt (1 1 1) and AgPt (1 1 1) crystal plane. It is reported that the electronic structure of the metal surface largely determines the binding strength of an adsorbate[55]. Therefore, the Total Density of States (TDOS) were calculated to demonstrate the change of electronic structure for Ag, Pt, AgPt alloy systems. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the TDOS of AgPt alloy at Fermi energy (EF) is much higher than that of Ag, proving the catalytic activity of the AgPt alloy system would be enhanced because of a stronger binding between the metal and the adsorbate [55,56]. The TDOS and Partial Density of States (PDOS) of CO adsorbed to Pt (1 1 1) and AgPt (1 1 1) crystal plane were calculated as in Fig. 8b and c. The peak value of the TDOS of CO on AgPt (1 1 1) is higher than that CO on Pt (1 1 1), indicating the energy level of the system increased after doping Ag atoms, which is conducive to the adsorption of CO molecules. Meanwhile, the PDOS of CO on Pt (1 1 1) near EF level mainly consists of C 2p, O 2p, and Pt 5d orbital. The PDOS of CO on AgPt (1 1 1) mainly consists of C 2p, O 2p, Pt 5d, and Ag 4d orbital, also Ag and Pt atoms have a strong resonance in the range −6 eV to 1 eV, indicating there is a great interaction between Ag and Pt. Moreover, the high peak value of PDOS of Ag further indicates that AgPt alloy can enhance the interaction between Pt and CO [57]. Then, as shown in Fig. S4, the Electron Density Difference (EDD) analysis indicates that more noteworthy charge accumulates towards Pt atoms on AgPt, and faster charge depletion near to neighboring C than in CO adsorbed Pt, indicating the stronger electron transfer behavior between CO and AgPt alloy. Fig. 9 shows the Mulliken population analysis of CO adsorbed on Pt (1 1 1) and AgPt (1 1 1) crystal plane. The change in interacting C (0.13 ∼ 0.26 e), Pt1 (0.21 ∼ 0.07 e), and Pt2 (-0.05 ∼ -0.34 e) from Pt (1 1 1) to AgPt (1 1 1) is obvious. Here, the positive and negative values indicate the gained and lost charges. These results indicate that when the adsorption surface is AgPt (1 1 1) crystal plane, there are more charges transferred from Pt to C atoms, which will facilitate the adsorption of CO because of the chemical coupling between Ag and Pt. It is also confirmed by the bond length of the molecule after optimization analysis results in Fig. 9. The C-O bond lengths of CO adsorbed on Pt (1 1 1) and AgPt (1 1 1) crystal plane are 1.166 Å and 1.160 Å respectively, which are both larger than the theoretical value (1.129 Å). The above result shows that both structures can weaken the interaction between C and O atoms, but compared with the distance of Pt to CO molecule (2.090 and 2.092 Å) the AgPt alloy to CO molecule (1.860 and 1.863 Å) is smaller, indicating stronger adsorption between AgPt alloy and CO molecule [58]. Moreover, the adsorption energy of CO on the AgPt (1 1 1) crystal plane (2.78 eV) is higher than CO on Pt (1 1 1) crystal plane (0.21 eV) (Table 1 ), which is consistent with the above absorption tendency on Pt (1 1 1) and AgPt (1 1 1) surface. These results suggest that the ligand effect of doped Ag in Pt leads to stronger bonding of CO which would hinder the further oxidation of CO. However, according to the bifunctional mechanism, the ·OH absorbed on the Ag can oxidize CO to COOH (Eq. 2) and a stronger adsorption between CO and Pt can reduce the reaction barrier of CO to COOH. Under alkaline conditions, the formation of COOH is beneficial to the desorption of intermediate products on Pt surface (Eq. 3) to enhance the stability of the catalysts.
(2) Pt-(CO)ads + Ag-(OH)ads ⇋ Ag + Pt-(COOH)ads
(3) Pt-(COOH)ads + OH− → Pt-(OH)ads + HCOO−

In summary, the AgPt NPs with abundant dendrites have been synthesized by a facile one-pot strategy. Due to the high surface active areas and electronic effects, the optimized AgPt NPs electrocatalysts show above 11 times increase in mass activity for MOR and excellent stability compared to commercial Pt/C catalysts. N-GQDs with high specific surface area and high electron mobility increase the catalytic activity of AgPt alloy. The introduction of Ag facilitates the chemical desorption of middle products COads, thus more Pt active sites are released and further enhancing their MOR performance. Moreover, DFT calculations were operated to study the mechanism of CO adsorption on Pt (1 1 1) and AgPt (1 1 1) crystal planes. These AgPt NPs offer a promising high-efficiency MOR electrocatalyst for practical DMFCs applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial was sponsored by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21165016, and 21265018), the Science and Technology Support Projects of Gansu Province (Nos.1011GKCA025, and 1208RJZM289), the Postgraduate Research Funding Projects of Northwest Normal University (2020KYZZ001163) and Outstanding Graduate Student Innovation Star project of Gansu (2021CXZX-292).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2022.01.028.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
